extras.title: Physics — Extras
extras.intro: Topics beyond the G9 revision list. For curious minds and IB DP preview.

relativity.title: Special relativity — intro
relativity.body: Two postulates (Einstein, 1905): the laws of physics are the same in all inertial frames; the speed of light is constant for all observers (c ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s). Consequences: moving clocks run slow (time dilation), moving objects shorten (length contraction), and E = mc² (mass-energy equivalence).

quantum.title: Quantum mechanics — intro
quantum.body: Energy and matter come in discrete packets (quanta). Photon energy E = hf, where h ≈ 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s. Wave–particle duality: electrons and photons behave as both. The uncertainty principle: you cannot know position and momentum simultaneously with perfect accuracy.

thermo.title: Thermodynamics — laws
items: 0th law — if A is in thermal equilibrium with B, and B with C, then A with C. Defines temperature.
1st law — energy is conserved: ΔU = Q − W (heat added minus work done).
2nd law — entropy of an isolated system never decreases. Why heat flows from hot to cold.
3rd law — entropy approaches a constant as temperature → 0 K.

zeroth.name: Zeroth law of thermodynamics
zeroth.rule: If A is in thermal equilibrium with B, and B is in thermal equilibrium with C, then A and C are in thermal equilibrium. This DEFINES temperature: two objects in thermal equilibrium have the same temperature.
examples: A thermometer (A) reads body temperature (C) via a third object (B = the thermometer itself).
Two pots of water at 80°C don't exchange net heat when joined — same temperature.
If you put cold milk in hot coffee, after a while they reach equilibrium — defines a common temperature.
zeroth.tip: Called 'zeroth' because it was discovered AFTER the others, but is logically more fundamental — without it, you can't even define temperature.

first.name: First law (conservation of energy)
first.rule: Energy can be neither created nor destroyed, only TRANSFORMED. ΔU = Q − W. ΔU = change in internal energy. Q = heat added TO system. W = work done BY system. Heat in adds energy; work out removes it.
examples: Heat 1 L of water (Q = 4180 J): temperature rises 1°C (ΔU = +4180 J, no work done so W = 0).
Push piston into compressed gas: work done ON gas (W < 0 in this sign convention), gas heats up.
Burning petrol in a car: chemical energy → kinetic + heat. Total energy conserved.
Perpetual motion machines IMPOSSIBLE — they'd violate this law.
first.tip: Conservation of energy is one of the most-tested principles in physics. Mention 'energy is conserved' in any answer about transformations.

second.name: Second law — entropy increases
second.rule: Entropy (disorder) of an ISOLATED system never DECREASES. Heat flows naturally from HOT to COLD, not the other way. Some energy in any process is 'wasted' as low-grade heat.
examples: Drop ice in hot tea — ice melts, tea cools. Never the reverse.
Eggs scramble, never unscramble.
Engines can never be 100% efficient — some heat must be lost (Carnot's theorem).
Universe is gradually cooling and becoming more disordered — 'heat death' in trillions of years.
second.watchOut: Doesn't mean local entropy never decreases — fridges and life DO decrease local entropy by INCREASING entropy elsewhere (waste heat, atmospheric heating).

third.name: Third law — absolute zero
third.rule: The entropy of a perfect crystal approaches ZERO as temperature approaches absolute zero (0 K = −273.15°C). You cannot REACH 0 K — only approach it.
examples: Coldest temperature ever achieved in a lab: 38 picokelvin (NIST, 2021).
Below ~4 K, helium becomes a superfluid — flows without friction.
Superconductivity (zero electrical resistance) typically appears below ~100 K.
Absolute zero would mean ZERO molecular motion — quantum mechanics forbids this exactly (zero-point energy).
third.tip: 0°C = 273.15 K. 25°C = 298.15 K. Always add 273 (approximately) to convert Celsius to Kelvin. Kelvin scale starts at absolute zero.

astro.title: Astrophysics — basics
astro.body: Stars form from collapsing gas clouds. Fusion of hydrogen → helium in the core releases energy. Stars die as white dwarfs, neutron stars, or black holes depending on mass. The universe began ~13.8 billion years ago (Big Bang) — evidence from cosmic microwave background and expansion (Hubble).

renewable.title: Renewable energy — engineering
renewable.body: Solar PV — photons knock electrons free in silicon, generating current. Wind — turbines convert kinetic energy of moving air to electrical (P ∝ v³). Hydro — gravitational PE of water drives turbines. Tidal — predictable, twice daily. Geothermal — heat from Earth's core.

home.title: Physics Revision
home.subtitle: Grade 9 — Interactive Course
home.intro: Study topics, explore key terms, and experiment with interactive simulations. Everything you need for the MYP Physics 9 exam — in one calm, focused place.

forceMotion.title: Force & Motion
forceMotion.desc: Vectors, Newton's laws, momentum and braking.

forcesEnergy.title: Forces & Energy
forcesEnergy.desc: Hooke's law, pressure, density and energy transfer.

electricity.title: Electricity & Magnetism
electricity.desc: Ohm's law, circuits, sensors, power consumption.

wavesOptics.title: Waves & Optics
wavesOptics.desc: Waves, reflection, refraction, the EM spectrum.

commandTerms.title: Command Terms
commandTerms.intro: Command terms tell you exactly what an exam question is asking for. Read them carefully and answer accordingly.

state.meaning: Give a specific fact or piece of information.
state.how: Just give the fact. No explanation needed.
state.example: State the SI unit of force. → newton (N).

describe.meaning: Give a detailed account of what happens.
describe.how: Outline the events or features in order.
describe.example: Describe the motion of a parachutist before reaching terminal velocity.

explain.meaning: Give reasons for why or how something happens.
explain.how: Use 'because' and link cause to effect.
explain.example: Explain why a thicker wire has lower resistance.

compare.how: Use linking words: similarly, however, whereas.

evaluate.meaning: Weigh up strengths and weaknesses, then reach a judgement.
evaluate.example: Evaluate the use of nuclear power.

calculate.meaning: Work out a numerical answer using a formula.
calculate.how: Write the formula, substitute, calculate, give units.
calculate.example: Calculate the force on a 5 kg mass accelerating at 2 m/s².

forceMotion.title: Force and Motion
forceMotion.intro: We study how and why objects move, what makes them speed up or stop, and what happens when they collide.

scalarsVectors.name: Scalars vs Vectors
scalarsVectors.rule: Scalars have magnitude only (size). Vectors have BOTH magnitude AND direction. When combining, scalars add arithmetically (5+3=8), but vectors must be added geometrically — sometimes 5+3 = 4 if they oppose.
examples: Distance (scalar) = total path = 7 m. Displacement (vector) = straight-line from start to end = 5 m, direction NE.
Speed: 60 km/h. Velocity: 60 km/h heading NORTH.
Walking 3 m E then 4 m N: total distance = 7 m, but displacement = 5 m NE (Pythagoras).
Resultant of 10 N right + 4 N left = 6 N right (opposite directions subtract).
scalarsVectors.tip: If you see 'how far' or 'how fast' without direction — scalar. If you see direction (N, up, +x) — vector. ALWAYS use vectors in F=ma.

newtonLaws.name: Newton's Three Laws
newtonLaws.rule: 1st (Inertia): An object stays at rest or moves at constant velocity UNLESS a resultant force acts on it. 2nd: F = ma — resultant force = mass × acceleration. 3rd: For every action force, there is an EQUAL and OPPOSITE reaction force on a DIFFERENT object.
examples: 1st: A puck on frictionless ice keeps moving at constant velocity forever. Seatbelts use 1st law — your body keeps moving when the car stops.
2nd: 10 kg pushed with 30 N → a = 3 m/s². Same 30 N on 5 kg → a = 6 m/s² (lighter = bigger acceleration).
3rd: Rocket pushes gas DOWN → gas pushes rocket UP. Walking: foot pushes ground BACKWARDS, ground pushes foot FORWARDS.
newtonLaws.watchOut: Action/reaction pairs are on DIFFERENT objects — they don't cancel out. Don't confuse with equal-and-opposite forces in equilibrium (same object, two forces).

momentumImpulse.name: Momentum & Impulse
momentumImpulse.rule: Momentum: p = m·v (vector, kg·m/s). Impulse: J = F·Δt = Δp = m·Δv (force × time = change in momentum). Conservation: total momentum before = total momentum after a collision (closed system, no external force).
examples: Truck 5000 kg at 10 m/s: p = 50 000 kg·m/s. Bullet 0.01 kg at 500 m/s: p = 5 kg·m/s. Truck has 10 000× more.
Crash: car 1000 kg at 20 m/s hits wall, stops in 0.1 s. F = (1000·20)/0.1 = 200 000 N. With airbag (0.5 s): F = 40 000 N. 5× less.
Conservation: 10 kg ball at 5 m/s hits stationary 5 kg ball, sticks. v_after = 50/15 = 3.33 m/s.
Recoil: rifle pushes 0.01 kg bullet at 400 m/s. Rifle 4 kg recoils at v = 0.01·400/4 = 1 m/s.
momentumImpulse.tip: Increasing TIME of impact reduces force for same Δp — that's why airbags, crumple zones, bent knees when landing all SAVE LIVES.

motionGraphs.name: Motion graphs (interpretation)
motionGraphs.rule: Distance-time graph: gradient = speed; horizontal line = at rest; steeper = faster. Velocity-time graph: gradient = acceleration; horizontal = constant velocity; AREA UNDER CURVE = distance travelled.
examples: d-t graph: straight line gradient 5 m/s = constant speed. Curve gets steeper = accelerating. Curve gets shallower = decelerating.
v-t graph: line from (0,0) to (4, 20) means a = 5 m/s². Area = ½·4·20 = 40 m distance.
v-t flat at 15 m/s for 10 s = 150 m distance. Then line down to 0 over 6 s = ½·6·15 = 45 m. Total = 195 m, deceleration = 2.5 m/s².
Negative gradient on v-t = deceleration (slowing). Negative velocity = moving backwards.
motionGraphs.tip: Always check axes! distance-time ≠ velocity-time. Gradient of v-t = a. Area under v-t = s. Memorise this.

terminalVelocity.name: Terminal velocity
terminalVelocity.rule: When falling through a fluid (air, water), air resistance INCREASES with speed. Terminal velocity = constant speed when air resistance EQUALS weight (resultant force = 0, so a = 0).
examples: Skydiver in spread position: terminal velocity ~53 m/s (~190 km/h).
Skydiver in 'streamlined' dive: ~80 m/s.
Open parachute: drag suddenly LARGER than weight → DECELERATES until new (slower) terminal velocity ~5-7 m/s.
Raindrop falls at 8-10 m/s terminal velocity (else it would hit at 200 m/s and kill you!).
On the Moon (no atmosphere): NO terminal velocity. Falls accelerate forever.
terminalVelocity.tip: Stages: (1) Initial — weight > drag, accelerates. (2) Drag grows. (3) Drag = weight, terminal velocity reached. (4) Parachute: drag >> weight, decelerates. (5) New terminal velocity (slower).

stoppingDistance.name: Stopping distance — thinking + braking
stoppingDistance.rule: Total stopping distance = thinking distance + braking distance. Thinking distance ∝ speed. Braking distance ∝ speed². So doubling speed DOUBLES thinking distance but QUADRUPLES braking distance.
examples: At 30 mph (~13 m/s): thinking 9 m + braking 14 m = 23 m total (Highway Code).
At 60 mph (~27 m/s): thinking 18 m + braking 55 m = 73 m total. 3× as far (not 2×!).
At 70 mph: thinking 21 m + braking 75 m = 96 m total — that's 24 car lengths.
Wet road: braking distance DOUBLES (from reduced friction). Icy: 10× longer.
Alcohol/drugs/phone: thinking distance dramatically up (reaction 0.7 s → 1.5 s).
stoppingDistance.watchOut: Don't confuse thinking distance (driver responding, before brakes touched) with braking distance (after brakes applied). KE ∝ v², so braking distance ∝ v² (energy to dissipate).

scalar.name: Scalar
scalar.definition: A quantity with magnitude only and no direction.

vector.name: Vector
vector.definition: A quantity with both magnitude and direction.
vector.example: Velocity, force and displacement are vectors.

displacement.name: Displacement
displacement.definition: The shortest distance between the start and end point in a specific direction.
displacement.example: Walking 3 m east then 4 m north gives a displacement of 5 m NE.

acceleration.name: Acceleration
acceleration.definition: The rate of change of velocity over time.
acceleration.example: A car going from 0 to 20 m/s in 4 s accelerates at 5 m/s².

resultantForce.name: Resultant force
resultantForce.definition: The overall force acting on an object after all forces are combined.
resultantForce.example: 10 N right − 4 N left = 6 N right.

newton1.name: Newton's First Law
newton1.definition: Objects stay at rest or move uniformly unless acted on by a resultant force.
newton1.example: A book on a table stays still until pushed.

newton2.name: Newton's Second Law
newton2.definition: Acceleration depends on force and mass (F = m × a).
newton2.example: Doubling the force doubles the acceleration.

newton3.name: Newton's Third Law
newton3.definition: Every action force has an equal and opposite reaction force.
newton3.example: When you jump, you push down on Earth, Earth pushes you up.

momentum.name: Momentum
momentum.definition: The quantity of motion of an object (p = mv).
momentum.example: A heavy truck at low speed can have the same momentum as a light car at high speed.

impulse.name: Impulse
impulse.definition: The change in momentum caused by a force over time (J = FΔt).
impulse.example: A longer contact time gives a larger impulse for the same force.

terminalVelocity.name: Terminal velocity
terminalVelocity.definition: Constant speed reached when air resistance equals weight.
terminalVelocity.example: A skydiver reaches about 53 m/s in free fall.

reactionTime.name: Reaction time
reactionTime.definition: Time taken to respond to a stimulus.
reactionTime.example: A typical reaction time is 0.2–0.9 s.

brakingDistance.name: Braking distance
brakingDistance.definition: Distance travelled after brakes are applied.
brakingDistance.example: Increases with speed squared, longer on wet roads.

formulas.accelerationCaption: Acceleration: change in velocity over time

sim1.title: Newton's Second Law (F = ma)
sim1.desc: Adjust mass and applied force to see how acceleration changes.

sim2.title: Terminal Velocity
sim2.desc: Drop a skydiver and watch weight and air resistance balance out.

sim3.title: Momentum Collision
sim3.desc: Set two masses moving toward each other, then choose elastic or inelastic.

sim4.title: Reaction & Braking Distance
sim4.desc: Explore how speed, reaction time and road conditions change stopping distance.

simVectors.title: Vector Addition
simVectors.desc: Combine two forces tip-to-tail and read the resultant — magnitude and direction.

simGraphs.title: Motion Graphs (s-t, v-t, a-t)
simGraphs.desc: Switch between scenarios and watch all three motion graphs update together.
simGraphs.explainConstantV: Constant velocity: flat v-t line, zero a-t.
simGraphs.explainConstantA: Uniform acceleration: straight line v-t, flat a-t.
simGraphs.explainDecel: Uniform deceleration: v-t decreases linearly until rest, then stays zero.
simGraphs.distanceNote: Distance = area under the v-t graph.

forcesEnergy.title: Forces and Energy
forcesEnergy.intro: How forces stretch, push, lift and do work — and how pressure and density shape what floats, sinks, or holds together.

hookesLaw.name: Hooke's Law — springs & elasticity
hookesLaw.rule: For a spring (or elastic material) within its elastic limit: F = k·x, where F = force applied, k = spring constant (N/m), x = extension. Doubling force doubles extension — IF below elastic limit. Beyond limit: permanently deformed.
examples: Spring k = 50 N/m. F = 10 N: x = F/k = 0.2 m.
Extension 4 cm with 8 N: k = 8/0.04 = 200 N/m.
Graph F vs x: straight line through origin = obeys Hooke's Law.
Beyond limit: graph curves, plastic deformation, spring won't return to original.
Stiff spring (high k): hard to stretch. Weak spring (low k): stretches easily.
hookesLaw.watchOut: EXTENSION x, not total length. If spring is 30 cm naturally and 35 cm under load, x = 5 cm = 0.05 m, not 0.35 m.

work.name: Work, energy & power
work.rule: Work = force × distance (in direction of force): W = F·d (joules, J). Energy = ability to do work, same units. Power = work per second: P = W/t (watts, W). 1 W = 1 J/s.
examples: Lifting 10 kg through 2 m against gravity (g = 10): W = mg·h = 10·10·2 = 200 J.
Pulling 5 N over 8 m: W = 40 J.
Same lift in 4 s: P = 200/4 = 50 W. In 1 s: P = 200 W (more powerful).
Athlete climbing stairs: weight × height ÷ time = power output. 700 N up 3 m in 5 s → 420 W.
work.tip: Force must be in DIRECTION of motion. Carrying a bag horizontally — no work done (force vertical, motion horizontal!)

energyStores.name: Energy stores & transfers
energyStores.rule: Energy can be STORED (kinetic, gravitational PE, elastic PE, chemical, thermal, nuclear) and TRANSFERRED (mechanically, electrically, by heating, by radiation). Total energy is CONSERVED.
examples: Falling ball: gravitational PE → kinetic energy (transferred mechanically).
Burning fuel: chemical store → thermal store + light (transfer by radiation & heating).
Pendulum: PE ↔ KE swap each swing. Total constant (ignoring friction).
Useful KE formula: KE = ½mv². 1 kg at 4 m/s: KE = ½·1·16 = 8 J.
Useful gravitational PE: PE = mgh. 1 kg lifted 5 m (g=10): PE = 50 J.
energyStores.tip: Always specify the STORE (where energy is) and the PATH (how it moved). 'Heat' is a transfer, not a store — proper name is 'thermal energy store'.

pressure.name: Pressure
pressure.rule: Pressure = force per unit area: P = F/A (pascals, Pa). 1 Pa = 1 N/m². Same force, smaller area = greater pressure. Acts in ALL directions in a fluid.
examples: Person 600 N standing on one foot, area 200 cm² = 0.02 m²: P = 600/0.02 = 30 000 Pa.
Stilettos concentrate weight on tiny area → high pressure → dent floors.
Sharp knife: tiny edge area → high pressure → cuts.
Snow shoes spread weight over big area → low pressure → don't sink.
Tractor tyres are wide for the same reason: don't compact soil.
pressure.tip: Pressure ≠ force. Same force can give wildly different pressures. ALWAYS check units: cm² → m² (×10⁻⁴), and force in N.

density.name: Density & floating
density.rule: Density = mass per unit volume: ρ = m/V (kg/m³ or g/cm³). Water = 1000 kg/m³ = 1 g/cm³. An object FLOATS in a fluid if its density < fluid's density; SINKS if greater.
examples: Ice ρ = 920 kg/m³ < 1000 = floats in water.
Iron 7900 kg/m³ > 1000 = sinks. But iron ships float — because hollow shape makes AVERAGE density < water.
Helium 0.18 kg/m³ < air's 1.2 = balloons rise.
1 cm³ gold (ρ=19.3) weighs 19.3 g. 1 cm³ aluminium (ρ=2.7) weighs 2.7 g.
Density of water depends on temperature: max density at 4°C.
density.watchOut: Same mass, different volume = different density. A kg of feathers vs a kg of lead — same mass, but lead has TINY volume → high density.

fluidPressure.name: Fluid pressure & atmospheric
fluidPressure.rule: Pressure in a fluid at depth h: P = ρ·g·h. Increases LINEARLY with depth. Atmospheric pressure at sea level ≈ 101 325 Pa (1 atm) — caused by ~100 km of air above. Decreases with altitude.
examples: 10 m underwater: ρgh = 1000·10·10 = 100 000 Pa = ~1 atm extra. Total = 2 atm.
Mariana Trench (11 000 m): ~110 MPa (1100×atm).
Mount Everest (8848 m): atmospheric drops to ~30 kPa = ~30% of sea-level.
Aeroplanes at 10 000 m: cabin pressurised to ~75 kPa (about Denver, Colorado).
Submarines crushed by pressure if their hull fails at depth.
fluidPressure.tip: Hydraulic systems use Pascal's principle: pressure transmits equally in all directions. Press one piston small, lift heavy car with large piston (mechanical advantage).

contactForce.name: Contact force
contactForce.definition: A force acting when objects touch.

elasticity.name: Elasticity
elasticity.definition: Ability to return to original shape after stretching.

hookesLaw.name: Hooke's Law
hookesLaw.definition: Extension is proportional to force applied (F = k × x).
hookesLaw.example: Doubling the force doubles the extension — until the limit.

workDone.name: Work done
workDone.definition: Energy transferred when a force moves an object (W = F × d).
workDone.example: Lifting a 10 N bag by 2 m does 20 J of work.

energyTransfer.name: Energy transfer
energyTransfer.definition: Movement of energy between objects or systems.
energyTransfer.example: Chemical energy in fuel → thermal energy in the engine.

pressure.name: Pressure
pressure.definition: Force acting per unit area (P = F / A).
pressure.example: A sharp knife focuses force on a small area, increasing pressure.

density.name: Density
density.definition: Mass per unit volume (ρ = m / V).
density.example: Water is 1000 kg/m³; iron is 7870 kg/m³.

fluidPressure.name: Fluid pressure
fluidPressure.definition: Pressure exerted by liquids or gases.

sim1.title: Hooke's Law
sim1.desc: Stretch a spring and watch the linear F-x relationship until the elastic limit.
sim1.warning: Elastic limit exceeded — spring is permanently deformed.

sim2.title: Pressure (P = F / A)
sim2.desc: See how spreading the same force across different areas changes pressure.

sim3.title: Density & Floating
sim3.desc: Compare object density with fluid density to predict floating or sinking.

sim4.title: Fluid Pressure with Depth
sim4.desc: Sink a probe into a fluid and watch hydrostatic pressure grow.

simEnergy.title: Energy Transformations (Pendulum)
simEnergy.desc: Watch potential and kinetic energy trade places as the pendulum swings.

simWorkPower.title: Work and Power
simWorkPower.desc: Lift a mass to a height in a chosen time — see the work done and power required.

electricity.title: Electricity and Magnetism
electricity.intro: Charge, current, resistance and circuits — plus the sensors and safety devices that put them to use.

currentVoltageResistance.name: Current, voltage & resistance — the trinity
currentVoltageResistance.rule: Current (I, amps) is the flow rate of charge: I = Q/t. Voltage (V, volts) is the energy given per coulomb of charge: V = E/Q. Resistance (R, ohms) is how much a component opposes current. Ohm's Law links them: V = IR.
examples: 1 A current = 1 coulomb of charge per second passing a point.
Battery 12 V means each coulomb gains 12 J energy as it flows.
A 4 Ω resistor with 2 A through it: V = 2·4 = 8 V across it.
Same resistor with 12 V across: I = V/R = 12/4 = 3 A.
Current is measured by ammeter IN SERIES; voltage by voltmeter IN PARALLEL with the component.
currentVoltageResistance.tip: Think water analogy: voltage = pump pressure (energy per drop), current = flow rate, resistance = pipe narrowness. Same intuition works.

ohmsLaw.name: Ohm's Law (V = IR)
ohmsLaw.rule: For ohmic conductors (fixed temperature): current is DIRECTLY proportional to voltage. V/I = R is constant. Graph V vs I is a straight line through origin. Filament lamps, diodes are NON-ohmic.
examples: A 5 Ω resistor at 10 V: I = V/R = 10/5 = 2 A.
Doubling voltage doubles current (same R).
Filament lamp: as I rises, filament heats up, R rises → V vs I curves (not straight line).
Diode: only conducts above ~0.7 V (forward), basically infinite R reverse.
ohmsLaw.watchOut: Ohm's Law ONLY holds at constant temperature. For a lamp/wire that gets hot, R changes — V vs I curves.

seriesParallel.name: Series vs Parallel circuits
seriesParallel.rule: Series: components in ONE loop. Current SAME through each. Voltages ADD: V_total = V₁ + V₂. Resistances ADD: R = R₁ + R₂. Parallel: components in SEPARATE branches. Voltage SAME across each. Currents ADD: I_total = I₁ + I₂. 1/R = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂.
examples: 3 Ω + 6 Ω in series: R = 9 Ω. At 9 V: I = 1 A throughout.
3 Ω || 6 Ω parallel: 1/R = 1/3 + 1/6 = 1/2, so R = 2 Ω. At 9 V total: 9/3 = 3 A in 3Ω branch, 9/6 = 1.5 A in 6Ω, total 4.5 A.
Two identical resistors in parallel: R = R/2 (halved).
Christmas lights: traditional were series (one fails, all out). Modern parallel: one fails, rest stay on.
seriesParallel.tip: Series adds resistance (current path lengthened). Parallel reduces resistance (more paths for current). House wiring is parallel — voltage on every socket is 230 V.

power.name: Electrical power
power.rule: Power = energy per second. P = V·I = I²·R = V²/R (W, watts). Energy used: E = P·t (J or kWh). Domestic bill is in kWh: 1 kWh = 1000 W × 3600 s = 3.6 MJ.
examples: 3 kW kettle for 2 minutes: E = 3000 × 120 = 360 000 J = 0.1 kWh.
Heater 2 kW for 5 h: 10 kWh. At 30p/kWh = £3.
Same current through 4 Ω vs 8 Ω resistor: P = I²R, so 8 Ω dissipates DOUBLE the power.
Same VOLTAGE across them: P = V²/R, so 4 Ω dissipates DOUBLE (more current draws).
power.tip: Choose your formula: known V & I → VI. Known I & R → I²R. Known V & R → V²/R. All equivalent via Ohm's Law.

safety.name: Mains electricity & safety
safety.rule: Mains in UK: 230 V AC, 50 Hz. Three wires: live (brown, 230 V), neutral (blue, 0 V), earth (green/yellow, safety). Safety devices: fuse (breaks if current > rating), circuit breaker, RCD (residual current device — detects imbalance, trips in ms), earth wire.
examples: A toaster's metal case is earthed — if live touches case, large current flows to earth, fuse blows.
3 A fuse for lamps & TVs; 13 A for kettle, iron, heater.
RCD trips in 30 ms — fast enough to prevent fatal shock.
Double-insulated appliances (radio, hairdryer) have plastic casing — no earth needed (square symbol).
safety.watchOut: Wet hands + electricity = fatal. AC 100 mA through heart = death within seconds. ALWAYS turn off at the wall before working on a circuit.

magnetism.name: Magnetism & electromagnetism
magnetism.rule: A current in a wire creates a magnetic field. Right-hand grip rule: thumb in direction of current, fingers curl in direction of field. Coiling the wire (solenoid) amplifies field. Iron core inside coil makes an ELECTROMAGNET — strong & switchable.
examples: Compass deflects near a current-carrying wire (Oersted's discovery, 1820).
Solenoid acts like a bar magnet — N pole where field lines exit.
Electromagnet in a junk-yard crane lifts cars — turn off current to drop.
Maglev trains: levitate on repelling electromagnets, no friction with rails.
Motor: current in coil between magnets → force → rotation. Generator: rotation → induced EMF (Faraday's law).
magnetism.tip: Motors and generators are the same machine running in opposite directions. Both rely on the interaction of current and magnetic field.

current.name: Current
current.definition: Flow of electric charge (I, in amperes).
current.example: Measured with an ammeter in series.

voltage.name: Voltage
voltage.definition: Energy transferred per unit charge (V, in volts).
voltage.example: Measured with a voltmeter in parallel.

resistance.name: Resistance
resistance.definition: Opposition to current flow (R, in ohms).
resistance.example: Long thin wires have higher resistance.

charge.name: Charge
charge.definition: Property causing electrical attraction or repulsion.
charge.example: Like charges repel, opposite charges attract.

parallelCircuit.name: Parallel circuit
parallelCircuit.example: Same voltage on each branch; current splits.

ohmicConductor.name: Ohmic conductor
ohmicConductor.example: Straight V–I graph through the origin.

ldr.name: LDR
ldr.example: Resistance falls as light increases — used in streetlights.

staticElectricity.name: Static electricity
staticElectricity.example: Hair sticking to a balloon after rubbing.

fuse.name: Fuse
fuse.definition: Device that melts if current is too high.

circuitBreaker.name: Circuit breaker
circuitBreaker.definition: Safety switch that cuts off current automatically.
circuitBreaker.example: Can be reset, unlike a fuse.

efficiency.name: Efficiency
efficiency.definition: Ratio of useful output to total input.

kwh.name: Kilowatt-hour (kWh)
kwh.definition: Unit used to measure electricity usage.
kwh.example: 1 kWh = a 1000 W appliance running for 1 hour.

sim1.title: Ohm's Law (V = IR)
sim1.desc: Set voltage and resistance; current and power update live.

sim2.title: Circuit Builder — Series vs Parallel
sim2.desc: Compare bulbs in series and parallel. Toggle each bulb on or off.

sim3.title: Thermistor & LDR
sim3.desc: See how a thermistor responds to temperature and an LDR responds to light.

sim4.title: Power Consumption Calculator
sim4.desc: Estimate monthly energy and cost for everyday devices.

simIV.title: I-V Characteristics — Ohmic vs Non-ohmic
simIV.desc: Compare how current depends on voltage for a resistor, a filament lamp and a diode.

simMagnetic.title: Magnetic Fields
simMagnetic.desc: Visualise field lines and compass directions for a bar magnet, a current-carrying wire or a solenoid.

wavesOptics.title: Waves and Optics
wavesOptics.intro: From ripples on a pond to gamma rays — all waves share a few simple properties.

waveTypes.name: Transverse vs Longitudinal waves
waveTypes.rule: Transverse: oscillations PERPENDICULAR to direction of travel. Longitudinal: oscillations PARALLEL to direction of travel (compressions and rarefactions). Both transfer energy WITHOUT transferring matter.
examples: Transverse: light, water waves, waves on a string, ALL EM waves.
Longitudinal: sound waves (compressions of air), ultrasound, P-waves in earthquakes.
Slinky shaken side-to-side = transverse. Pushed forward-and-back = longitudinal.
Earthquake P-waves (longitudinal) arrive first, then S-waves (transverse, can't go through liquid core).
waveTypes.tip: Visualise an oscillating particle. Direction of oscillation vs direction of wave travel — perpendicular (T) or parallel (L)?

waveEquation.name: Wave equation: v = f·λ
waveEquation.rule: Speed (v, m/s) = frequency (f, Hz) × wavelength (λ, m). Period T = 1/f. The 'time to make one full oscillation' is the period; 'how many oscillations per second' is the frequency.
examples: Sound 340 m/s at frequency 1000 Hz: λ = 0.34 m.
Red light 4.3 × 10¹⁴ Hz, in vacuum v = 3 × 10⁸ m/s: λ = 700 nm.
FM radio at 100 MHz: λ = c/f = 3 m wavelength.
Period of mains AC (50 Hz): T = 1/50 = 0.02 s = 20 ms.
waveEquation.tip: When wave passes from one medium to another, FREQUENCY stays the same (source unchanged) but SPEED & WAVELENGTH change.

emSpectrum.name: Electromagnetic spectrum
emSpectrum.rule: All EM waves travel at c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s in vacuum. Order from longest wavelength to shortest: Radio → Microwave → Infrared → Visible (Red-Orange-Yellow-Green-Blue-Indigo-Violet) → Ultraviolet → X-ray → Gamma. Higher frequency = more energy.
examples: Radio: communication, TV, FM/AM. λ ~ m to km.
Microwaves: cooking (water absorbs 2.4 GHz), wifi, GPS.
Infrared: thermal cameras, remote controls. Felt as heat.
Visible: 400-700 nm. Our eyes detect this narrow band only.
UV: tans skin, vitamin D, sterilises water. Causes sunburn, cancer.
X-ray: medical imaging, security scans. Ionises atoms.
Gamma: radioactive decay, nuclear medicine. Most dangerous: penetrates and ionises.
emSpectrum.watchOut: Higher frequency = MORE energy = more biologically damaging. UV and above are IONISING (knock electrons out of atoms).

reflection.name: Reflection
reflection.rule: Angle of incidence = angle of reflection. Both measured from the NORMAL (perpendicular line to surface), not from the surface itself. Smooth surface = specular (mirror-like). Rough = diffuse (scattered).
examples: Mirror reflection: ray strikes at 30° from normal → reflects at 30° from normal.
Image in plane mirror: virtual (behind mirror), same size as object, same distance behind as object is in front, LATERALLY INVERTED.
Periscope: two parallel mirrors at 45°.
Why white paper looks white in all directions: diffuse reflection of white light.
Calm lake = mirror-like reflection. Rough sea = diffuse.
reflection.tip: ALWAYS draw the normal first (dashed line). Measure angles FROM the normal, not from the surface.

refraction.name: Refraction
refraction.rule: When a wave enters a different medium, its SPEED changes — and if it strikes at an angle, it BENDS. Going from less dense to more dense (air → glass): bends TOWARDS normal. Glass → air: bends AWAY from normal.
examples: Pencil in water looks bent at surface — light refracted.
Lenses focus light by refraction: converging lens — parallel rays meet at focal point.
Prism splits white light: different colours refract by different amounts (dispersion).
Total internal reflection (TIR): light from glass to air at large angle (>critical angle) reflects ENTIRELY. Used in optical fibres (data travels 1000s km this way).
Mirages: hot air near road bends light, creating 'water'.
refraction.tip: Speed changes drive bending. Frequency stays constant; wavelength shortens in slower medium.

sound.name: Sound waves & hearing
sound.rule: Sound = longitudinal wave; needs a medium (no sound in vacuum). Speed in air ~340 m/s, water ~1500 m/s, steel ~5000 m/s (denser/more rigid = faster). Human hearing: ~20 Hz to ~20 kHz. Below = infrasound (elephants, whales); above = ultrasound (bats, medical imaging).
examples: Pitch = frequency. Higher freq = higher pitch.
Volume = amplitude. Larger amp = louder. Measured in decibels (dB) — logarithmic scale.
Thunder/lightning gap: 3 s = ~1 km away (sound travels 340 m/s vs light essentially instantly).
Ultrasound: 2-18 MHz, used for foetal imaging, kidney stones, NDT.
Doppler effect: pitch rises as source approaches (compressed waves), falls as it recedes (stretched waves).
sound.tip: Sound is mechanical and requires a medium. EM waves don't (light travels through space).

amplitude.name: Amplitude
amplitude.example: Higher amplitude = louder sound, brighter light.

wavelength.name: Wavelength
wavelength.definition: Distance between two matching points on waves (λ).

frequency.name: Frequency
frequency.definition: Number of waves passing per second (f, in Hz).
frequency.example: Audible sound is about 20 Hz–20 kHz.

emSpectrum.name: Electromagnetic spectrum
emSpectrum.example: Radio, micro, IR, visible, UV, X-ray, gamma.

reflection.name: Reflection
reflection.definition: Bouncing of waves from a surface.
reflection.example: Angle of incidence = angle of reflection.

refraction.name: Refraction
refraction.definition: Bending of waves entering another medium.
refraction.example: A straw looks bent in a glass of water.

diffraction.name: Diffraction
diffraction.definition: Spreading of waves through gaps or around obstacles.

filter.name: Filter
filter.example: A red filter blocks all except red light.

formulas.snell: n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂

sim1.title: Wave Explorer
sim1.desc: Tune amplitude, wavelength and frequency. Switch transverse or longitudinal.

sim2.title: Reflection & Refraction
sim2.desc: Shine a ray at a boundary and watch Snell's law in action.

sim3.title: Diffraction
sim3.desc: Pass a plane wave through a gap and watch it spread.

sim4.title: Electromagnetic Spectrum
sim4.desc: Slide along the spectrum to explore each region.

sim5.title: Converging Lens
sim5.desc: Move the object and watch the image form through three principal rays.

simSound.title: Sound Wave — Pitch & Loudness
simSound.desc: Tune frequency (pitch) and amplitude (loudness) and see the wave + air-particle compressions.

simFilters.title: Colour & Filters
simFilters.desc: Shine a coloured light through a filter onto an object and predict what you see.

criteria.title: MYP Criteria
criteria.intro: Four criteria are assessed in MYP Physics. Use the checklists to make sure your response covers everything.

A.title: Criterion A — Knowing & Understanding
A.subtitle: Recall and apply scientific knowledge (the written exam)
items: Recall scientific facts, terms and definitions accurately — vocabulary like 'momentum', 'resultant force', 'refraction' must be used precisely.
Apply scientific knowledge to solve problems in familiar situations (e.g. calculate F = ma when force and mass are given).
Apply scientific knowledge to solve problems in unfamiliar situations (transfer the same idea to a new context, e.g. why an airbag works).
Analyse and evaluate scientific information to make scientifically supported judgements.
Show your working in calculations: formula → substitution → answer → units.
Use the correct command term (state, describe, explain, compare, evaluate, calculate) when answering.

B.title: Criterion B — Inquiring & Designing
B.subtitle: Planning a lab
items: Clear research question with independent, dependent and controlled variables.
Hypothesis with scientific reasoning ('If… then… because…').
Materials and apparatus list with sizes / quantities.
Numbered method that another student could repeat exactly.
Safety considerations identified.
Plan for collecting enough data (≥ 5 values, ≥ 3 repeats).

C.title: Criterion C — Processing & Evaluating
C.subtitle: Analyzing a lab
items: Data table with units in headers and consistent decimal places.
Averages, ranges and uncertainties calculated.
Graph: title, labelled axes, suitable scale, line of best fit.
Trend described in words and linked to the science.
Conclusion answers the research question with evidence.
Evaluation: strengths, weaknesses and realistic improvements.

D.title: Criterion D — Reflecting on the Impacts of Science
D.subtitle: The essay
items: Clear scientific explanation of the issue.
Discussion of social, economic and environmental impacts.
Multiple perspectives considered (for / against / neutral).
Evidence from at least two reliable sources.
Personal opinion with justification.
References listed in a consistent format.

resources.title: Resources
resources.intro: External revision sites and references — keep them open while you study.

bbc.desc: Short topic notes and quick quizzes.

practice.intro: MYP exam-style questions on this topic. Try answering first using the command term, then reveal the model answer.

footer.noTracking: No accounts, no tracking — your progress lives only in this browser.
